text: The robotic system begins the docking process, moving forward and autonomously turning to the patient's right for lateral docking. The robot and instruments are positioned and ready for the procedure.
The robotic system is seen moving back to its original position, preparing for the demonstration of the surgical instruments.

The video shows a typical ATMM trocar of the robotic system and a standard instrument. The instrument is inserted into the trocar, similar to laparoscopic instruments, and is capable of rotating 360 degrees.

The robotic fingers demonstrate their ability to move in multiple angles around the human finger, highlighting their flexibility compared to human movement. A sterile drape is applied as the trocar is prepared for docking.
The surgeon docks the trocar by pressing it into place, followed by installing the endoscope for targeting. The robotic system provides instructions, and an instrument is inserted into the trocar.
The robotic arm moves the instrument down to its desired position. The surgeon demonstrates changing the instrument by pressing a button, allowing the instrument to be released and removed.

The surgeon removes one instrument and inserts another into the robotic arm. The instrument is recognized, indicated by a flashing green light, which turns blue once the instrument is fully inserted and ready for use.
The robotic instrument returns to its original position and demonstrates its ability to move in various angles, showcasing nine degrees of freedom through multiple directional controls.
The robotic arms and instruments inside the abdomen move in response to the surgeon's hand movements at the console, demonstrating the translation of external controls to internal surgical actions.
